sections.title: Expert WCAG Compliance Consultants in US
sections.description:
Looking to make your website fully accessible and ADA-compliant? Accessibility Innovations is your trusted partner for WCAG compliance consulting across the U.S. Our certified consultants specialize in auditing, identifying, and fixing digital accessibility barriers based on the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG).

We help businesses, government agencies, nonprofits, and private organizations meet ADA, Section 508, and other U.S. accessibility requirements. Our goal is to make your website inclusive, user-friendly, and legally compliant—while enhancing user experience and expanding your reach.

Whether you need a basic audit or complete remediation, our WCAG compliance specialists are here to help.

image.alt: Business professional reviewing website accessibility reports on a computer screen, ensuring WCAG compliance for digital content.

sections.title: Understanding Web Content Accessibility Guidelines
sections.description:
The Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) are internationally recognized standards developed by the W3C (World Wide Web Consortium). Their goal is to ensure digital content—such as websites, mobile apps, and PDFs—is accessible to users with a wide range of disabilities, including visual, auditory, motor, and cognitive impairments.

WCAG is organized around four key principles: content must be Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, and Robust (POUR). The guidelines offer three levels of conformance:

  • Level A: Basic accessibility
  • Level AA: The legal standard for most organizations
  • Level AAA: The highest level, often optional

In the United States, many ADA-related lawsuits and Department of Justice (DOJ) guidance refer to WCAG—especially WCAG 2.1 and 2.2—as the standard benchmark for digital accessibility.

sections.title: Priority Key Areas Covered Under WCAG
sections.description: We help organizations address the most critical accessibility elements required by WCAG. Our audits and services cover the following areas:

items.title: Accessible Navigation
items.description: Ensuring all users can move through your website using a keyboard, screen reader, or other assistive technology.

items.title: Readable & Understandable Content
items.description: Structuring content using clear headings, plain language, and predictable design for ease of comprehension.

items.title: Interactive Component Compliance
items.description: Making forms, buttons, dropdowns, and sliders accessible to users with assistive tech and alternative input methods.

items.title: Contrast & Visual Accessibility
items.description: Ensuring sufficient color contrast and scalable text to accommodate users with visual impairments.

items.title: Assistive Tech Compatibility
items.description: Testing compatibility with screen readers (JAWS, NVDA), voice input tools, and browser-based accessibility settings.

items.title: Accessibility Audits & Certifications

items.title: Remediation Roadmaps

items.title: Policy Development & Governance
items.description: Assistance with writing accessibility policies, setting internal standards, and embedding accessibility into your organizational culture.

items.title: Training & Coaching

items.title: Ongoing Accessibility Support

items.question: What does WCAG compliant mean?
items.answer: It means your website or digital content meets the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines, ensuring accessibility for people with disabilities.

items.question: What is the WCAG compliance law?
items.answer: WCAG isn't a law but is the standard referenced by the ADA and Section 508 to measure digital accessibility compliance.

items.question: What happens if you are not WCAG compliant?
items.answer: You risk ADA lawsuits, penalties, reputational damage, and excluding users with disabilities.

items.question: What is the difference between WCAG and ADA compliant?
items.answer: The ADA is the law, while WCAG provides the technical guidelines to achieve compliance.

items.question: Is WCAG 2.0 a legal requirement in the USA?
items.answer: Not directly, but courts and the DOJ use WCAG 2.0 and 2.1 as benchmarks for ADA compliance.

items.question: What is the most common WCAG failure?
items.answer: Frequent failures include missing alt text, poor color contrast, and lack of keyboard accessibility.

items.question: How do I make my website WCAG compliant?
items.answer: Audit your site, fix barriers, test with assistive tech, and consider expert accessibility remediation services. Contact us today to get Started with WCAG compliance.
